agenda_briefs.title: Council to discuss closed-door legal strategy in Carle v. City of Richmond lawsuit
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to hold a closed-door meeting with legal counsel to discuss an existing lawsuit against the city. The case is called Carle v. City of Richmond (Contra Costa County Superior Court Case No. C24-03531). This is a legal proceeding where someone named Carle is suing the city. Council will meet privately to talk about the city's legal strategy and options. No decision on the lawsuit itself will be made at this meeting — it is a discussion about how to handle the case. The meeting is allowed under a state law (Government Code Section 54956.9) that lets councils discuss ongoing litigation in private.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
C.1
CONFERENCE WITH LEGAL COUNSEL - EXISTING LITIGATION (paragraph (1) of Subdivision [d] of Government Code Section 54956.9)
Public Comments:
Carle v. City of Richmond
, Contra Costa County Superior Court Case No. C24-03531

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond Council to negotiate sale of Miraflores property on South 45th Street
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond Council is set to vote on whether to authorize closed-door negotiations with Acrire Holdings LLC for the sale of the Miraflores property on South 45th Street. The proposed deal would involve the City Manager and other officials negotiating the price and payment terms. This is a preliminary step — no sale has been approved yet, and the public will not be present during the negotiation discussions.
agenda_briefs.action_required: No action required from you at this time. Watch for the council decision — if approved, this could affect nearby property values or development plans.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
C.2
CONFERENCE WITH REAL PROPERTY NEGOTIATOR (Government Code Section 54956.8)
Public Comments:
Property: Miraflores property, South 45th Street
Agency Negotiator: Shasa Curl (City Manager), Lina Velasco (Community Development Director), Nannette Beacham (Economic Development Director Attorney)
Negotiating Parties: Acrire Holdings LLC
Under Negotiation: Price, Terms of Payment

agenda_briefs.title: Council to consider labor negotiations with multiple employee unions
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on whether to authorize labor negotiations with several employee organizations representing City of Richmond workers. A proposed closed-session meeting would allow council to discuss strategy with its labor negotiators (Sharrone Taylor, Jack Hughes, and Lisa Charbonneau) before bargaining begins. The affected groups include SEIU Local 1021 (full-time and part-time units), IFPTE Local 21 (mid-level and executive management units), the Richmond Police Officers Association (RPOA), the Richmond Police Management Association (RPMA), IAFF Local 188 (firefighters), and the Richmond Fire Management Association (RFMA). This is a preliminary step — no contracts or terms have been approved yet.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
C.3
CONFERENCE WITH LABOR NEGOTIATORS (Government Code Section 54957.6)
Public Comments:
Agency Representatives: Sharrone Taylor, Jack Hughes, and Lisa Charbonneau
Employee organizations:
SEIU Local 1021 Full Time Unit and Part Time Unit
IFPTE Local 21 Mid-Level Management Unit and Executive Management Units
Richmond Police Officers Association RPOA
Richmond Police Management Association RPMA
IAFF Local 188
Richmond Fire Management Association RFMA

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond Council to vote on iManage cloud contract extension
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond Council is set to vote on a proposed second contract amendment with iManage LLC for cloud-based legal document management services. If approved, the amendment would increase the total contract payment limit to $73,680.01 and extend the contract term from July 1, 2026, through September 30, 2027. The annual cost would increase from $24,000 to $25,680.01. Funding is from the approved FY 2025-2026 City Attorney's Office budget (Account String: 01151014-400209). The contractor must maintain a City of Richmond business license per Municipal Code Section 7.04.030.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond to name delegate for California Cities conference
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote on a proposal to designate Vice Mayor Doria Robinson as Richmond's voting delegate for the League of California Cities' 2026 Annual Conference, to be held September 23–25, 2026 at the Anaheim Convention Center. The deadline to submit delegate and alternate names is September 1, 2026. Both delegate and alternate must be registered for the conference. Travel, registration, and hotel costs will be covered from existing travel budgets.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond Council to vote on $266,514 contract for Miraflores park work
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond Council will vote on a fourth contract amendment with CSW/ST2, increasing the contract by $266,514 to a total of $637,941.50 for landscape architectural services at Miraflores. The work includes redesign of the Japanese American Confinement Sites interpretive signage (signs to be installed by August 2026), rehabilitation of historic structures (including reconstruction of the fire-damaged Sakai House), and annual Baxter Creek post-project monitoring. Council will also accept a $50,000 Rotary Club of Richmond grant from 2019 for the signage component. The contract runs until June 30, 2028.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: North Richmond waste fee spending plan for FY 2026-27
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider approving the Fiscal Year 2026-2027 North Richmond Waste and Recovery Mitigation Fee Expenditure Plan. This plan lists the activities that would be paid for using money collected from waste-related fees (called 'Mitigation Fees') in the North Richmond area. The plan covers the period from July 1, 2026, to June 30, 2027. It was recommended by a joint committee of local officials. If approved, this would decide how those fee dollars are spent for the year. The plan decreases funding for Strategy 2 (Neighborhood Clean-ups) and decreases contingency funding; all other strategies remain unchanged. The County Board of Supervisors already approved the plan on June 23, 2026.

key_dates.label: Next Mitigation Committee meeting

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond council to vote on EMS data contract with Definitive Networks
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond City Council will vote on approving a sole-source contract with Definitive Networks, Inc. for EMS data reporting and records management support. The three-year contract, effective July 1, 2025 through June 30, 2028, costs up to $216,834, with two optional one-year extensions at $63,917 each. The contract covers CQI review of Zoll ePCR reports, ImageTrend setup and admin support, and access to FireGauge CQI module. Funding is from General Fund and EMS Fund (Measure H).

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond council to vote on homeless outreach contract with Contra Costa County
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed three-year contract with Contra Costa County's Coordinated Outreach, Referral, and Engagement (CORE) program for homeless outreach services. If approved, the contract would expand outreach to six days per week and add housing stability resources. The total cost would not exceed $1,660,150, covering July 1, 2026, through June 30, 2029. This is a proposed decision — it has not been approved yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — if approved, this contract could affect local businesses near outreach areas.

agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on a resolution to re-certify the city's updated Sewer System Management Plan (SSMP). The plan must be submitted to the State Water Resources Control Board by August 2, 2025. The update cycle has changed from every five years to every six years. The plan is managed by Veolia Water under contract to the city.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond council to vote on stormwater fee agreement with county
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed agreement with the Contra Costa County Flood Control and Water Conservation District. If approved, this would allow the city to keep collecting stormwater fees from property owners and businesses. The money would be used for storm drain maintenance and to meet state water pollution rules. This is not a new fee — it renews an existing program that was set to expire.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — if approved, stormwater fees on your property bill will continue as before.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: New rules for temporary sewage holding tanks in Richmond
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider a proposed bylaw that would create new construction standards and a permitting process for temporary sewage holding tanks. This would apply to properties that cannot connect to the city's main sewer system. The proposal is up for a first reading and has not been approved yet.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond to vote on sanitary and storm fee collection for 2026-27
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will hold a public hearing to consider adopting a resolution that would allow the city to collect sanitary sewer and stormwater fees by adding them to the annual property tax rolls for the 2026-27 fiscal year. This is a proposed change — it has not been approved yet. The fees would cover the cost of running the sewer and stormwater systems. A 'resolution' is a formal decision by council; putting fees on the 'tax rolls' means they would be collected along with property taxes, not as a separate bill. Rates remain unchanged from FY 2024-25: single-unit residential $1,121/SSU sanitary + $32/SSU storm; multi-unit residential $964/SSU sanitary + $32/SSU storm; commercial/industrial $87/IWU + $1.841/lb BOD + $0.859/lb TSS (min. $671/facility). Total collection: ~$32.75M wastewater + ~$1.975M stormwater.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Attend the public hearing on July 7, 2026, or submit written objections/protests to the City Clerk by July 3, 2026 at 4:00 p.m., including property address, APN, and owner name/mailing address.

key_dates.label: Written protest deadline — Richmond sanitary/storm fee proposal
Public hearing — Richmond sanitary/storm fee proposal

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond Council to vote on supporting cleanup plan for United Heckathorn Superfund site
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond City Council will vote on a resolution endorsing a community cleanup plan for the 20-acre United Heckathorn Superfund site (5 acres land, 15 acres marine sediment in Lauritzen Channel and Parr Canal). The plan calls for isolating and draining the channel, removing all contaminated materials, constructing a groundwater collection and treatment system, offsite disposal, and post-cleanup monitoring. The EPA is expected to release its own cleanup proposal for public comment by late August 2026. The resolution has no financial impact on the city.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Contact Councilmember Sue Wilson (510-926-0408), Vice Mayor Doria Robinson (510-620-6568), or Councilmember Cesar Zepeda (510-620-6593) before the vote.

key_dates.label: EPA releases proposed cleanup plan for public comment

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on Richmond Police's 2025 military equipment use report
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond City Council will vote on approving the Richmond Police Department's 2025 Annual Military Equipment Use Report, covering January 1–December 31, 2025. If approved, the resolution authorizes continued use of equipment including unmanned aerial systems, tactical robots, mobile command center, armored rescue carrier, breaching shotguns, rifles, diversionary devices, chemical agents, 40mm launchers, less lethal shotguns, and less lethal launchers. The report includes new patrol rifle deployment tracking, demographic data collection, and policy updates on armored rescue vehicles from other agencies. No fiscal impact is associated with this item.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for council decision on July 7, 2026 — if approved, police will continue using listed military equipment for another year.

key_dates.label: Council vote on 2025 military equipment report

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond council to vote on $49,999 housing consultant contract amendment
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote on a proposed three-year, $216,834 sole-source contract with Definitive Networks, Inc. for Emergency Medical Services data reporting and records management compliance support. The contract would run from July 1, 2025 through June 30, 2028, with two one-year extension options at $63,917 per year. Annual costs are $89,000 (FY25-26), $63,917 (FY26-27), and $63,917 (FY27-28). The Fire Department lacks qualified personnel and infrastructure to meet state and federal data reporting requirements, including the new National Emergency Response Information System (NERIS).
agenda_briefs.action_required: Contact your Richmond city councillor before the council vote to express your views on this proposed contract.

key_dates.label: Council vote on Definitive Networks contract

agenda_briefs.source_body:
Q.3
Contract Amendment No. 2 with Just Cities, LLC for completion of the Housing Equity Roadmap as specified in the Housing Element
Attachments
|
Public Comments
1.
Cont. from 6-16, Just Cities, LLC Amendment No. 2.pdf
2.
Attachment 1 - Just Cities - Amend. No. 2.pdf
3.
Attachment 2 - Original Contract and Amend. No. 1.pdf
Claudia Citroen
Follow up on the initial FORMAL OBJECTION TO CONSENT CALENDAR PLACEMENT W.3.c, REQUEST FOR REMOVAL, AND DEMAND FOR FULL PUBLIC DISCUSSION

The proposed Contract Amendment No. 2 with Just Cities, LLC should be removed from the Consent Calendar and placed on the regular agenda for full public discussion, staff presentation, Council deliberation, and a separate recorded vote.

This item is neither routine nor ministerial. The staff report explicitly states that the Housing Equity Roadmap (HER) will guide future City policies, programs, investments, implementation strategies, housing initiatives, fair housing actions, land-use decisions, and equitable development efforts. These are substantial public policy matters with potentially significant long-term fiscal, legal, and community impacts.

The City seeks to increase the contract by $49,999 and extend the consultant relationship through December 31, 2027, while authorizing continued implementation assistance for policies and recommendations developed by the same consultant. This is not a simple administrative amendment. It represents an expansion of consultant involvement in shaping and advancing future City housing policy.

The staff report further acknowledges that the Housing Equity Roadmap addresses issues including racial segregation, housing production, land-use compatibility, fair housing access, housing habitability, equitable development, and future public investments. These subjects are among the most consequential policy issues facing Richmond and warrant robust public discussion rather than summary approval through a single Consent Calendar vote.

Particularly concerning is the proposed increase of $49,999—an amount that falls precisely one dollar below the commonly recognized $50,000 threshold that often triggers heightened review, reporting, or approval scrutiny under municipal purchasing and contracting practices. While the City may assert compliance with applicable procedures, the proximity of the requested amount to that threshold reasonably raises questions regarding contract structuring, oversight, fiscal transparency, and whether the public is receiving the level of scrutiny expected for expenditures of this nature.

Additionally, Just Cities, LLC prepared the City's original Affirmatively Furthering Fair Housing (AFFH) analysis, subsequently developed the Housing Equity Roadmap, and now seeks additional compensation to assist in implementation of recommendations arising from its own work product. This revolving consultant role raises legitimate questions regarding independent review, performance evaluation, measurable deliverables, consultant accountability, procurement practices, and the absence of competitive alternatives.

Approval of this matter through the Consent Calendar creates the appearance of minimizing public examination of a significant and potentially controversial policy initiative. Such action may be inconsistent with fundamental principles of transparency, accountability, and meaningful public participation that underlie California's open-government framework.

Richmond residents are entitled to understand:

• The total amount paid to Just Cities, LLC under all agreements and amendments;

• The specific deliverables completed to date;

• The measurable outcomes achieved over the previous two years of work;

• The justification for extending the contract through 2027;

• Whether alternative vendors or competitive procurement options were considered;

• What specific implementation activities are contemplated under this amendment;

• What future policy recommendations may result from this work; and

• What fiscal impacts may ultimately arise from implementation of the Housing Equity Roadmap.

The public cannot reasonably evaluate these questions when the matter is buried within the Consent Calendar and voted upon alongside routine administrative items.

Accordingly, residents request that this item be immediately removed from the Consent Calendar and placed on the regular agenda for:

A full staff presentation;
Complete disclosure of prior expenditures and consultant performance;
Public comment and questioning;
Council deliberation on the record; and
A separate roll-call vote.
Major policy initiatives, consultant-driven implementation strategies, and additional expenditures of taxpayer funds deserve full public scrutiny. Transparency requires discussion. Accountability requires deliberation. Public trust requires both.
APPROVE a second contract amendment with Just Cities, LLC to support completion and implementation of the Housing Equity Road Map increasing the payment limit by $49,999, for a total payment limit of $209,994, and extending the term through December 31, 2027– Community Development Department (Lina Velasco 510-620-6841/Jesus Morales 510-620-6649).

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Council to vote on $87,777 graffiti removal contract for historic building
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond Council is set to vote on a proposed contract with All Star Painting KD Inc. to remove graffiti from the historic concrete surfaces of the General Warehouse Building at Point Potrero Marine Terminal. If approved, the contract would run from June 1, 2026 to December 30, 2027, and cost no more than $87,777. Work includes graffiti removal from all exterior concrete surfaces, doors, windows, and loading docks, plus application of anti-graffiti coating to approximately 15,000 square feet. All work must adhere to the Secretary of the Interior's Standards for Historic Properties; abrasive blasting, sandblasting, pressure washing over 500 psi, grinding, and wire brushing are prohibited. The contractor must complete all work within 20 working days of the Notice to Proceed.

key_dates.label: Council vote on graffiti removal contract for General Warehouse Building

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond Council to vote on Glenn & McBryde Ave road closure for park safety
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed roadway modification that would close the direct vehicle connection between Glenn Avenue and McBryde Avenue as part of the McBryde Avenue Safe Routes to Park Project. The goal is to improve safety for pedestrians and cyclists and reduce cut-through traffic. However, a public comment raises serious legal concerns, arguing the city has not done required environmental reviews under the California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA), including studies on traffic diversion, emergency access, vehicle miles traveled, and alternatives. The comment warns that approving the closure without proper studies could expose the city to lawsuits and court orders to stop the project. Council will consider adopting a resolution to approve the modification at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision date and consider submitting your comments on the proposed road closure before the vote.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
Q.5
Roadway Modification at Glenn Avenue and McBryde Avenue for the McBryde Avenue Safe Routes to Park Project
Attachments
|
Public Comments
1.
Continued from 6-16, Roadway Modifications at Glenn Ave and McBryde Ave.pdf
2.
Attachment 1 - Location Map.pdf
3.
Attachment 2 - Conceptual Improvement Exhibit.pdf
4.
Attachment 3 - City of San Pablo Letter of Support.pdf
5.
Attachment 4 - Resolution.pdf
6.
Attachment 5 - Presentation and Community Engagement Images Notes Glenn_Avenue_Council_Final.pdf
Claudia Citroen
HALT THE PROJECT IMMEDIATELY!!
Standard Review Request:

Approval of this roadway closure without a complete and legally adequate environmental and planning record exposes the City to CEQA litigation risk, including injunctive relief and potential invalidation.
- CEQA judicial review applies the “abuse of discretion” standard, including:
- Failure to proceed in the manner required by law
- Decisions unsupported by substantial evidence in the administrative record

->INSUFFICIENT EVIDENTIARY RECORD FOR SAFETY FINDINGS
- No quantified collision history provided
- No speed study data provided
- No modeled traffic diversion impacts provided
- No baseline or before/after safety analysis
- Safety improvement claims are unsupported by technical evidence

-> TRAFFIC DIVERSION AND PUBLIC SAFETY IMPACTS
- Closure will shift traffic to surrounding residential and collector streets
- No identification of impacted corridors
- No peak-hour congestion analysis
- No emergency response routing analysis (fire/EMS/police)

-> EMERGENCY ACCESS REQUIREMENTS
- No emergency access study included
- No response time analysis provided
- No confirmation of functional alternate routes for emergency services

-> CALIFORNIA ENVIRONMENTAL QUALITY ACT (CEQA) REQUIREMENTS
CEQA applies to discretionary projects with potential physical environmental change:
- Pub. Resources Code § 21065 et seq.
- CEQA Guidelines § 15000 et seq.
- Roadway closure may affect:
Traffic patterns
Vehicle miles traveled (VMT)
Neighborhood circulation
Air quality and noise distribution
Environmental review or valid exemption must be supported by substantial evidence

-> CEQA “PROJECT” DETERMINATION (REQUIRED)
- Must explicitly determine whether action is a “project” under Pub. Resources Code § 21065
- Determination must be stated on the record before exemption reliance

-> EXEMPTION LIMITATIONS (CEQA GUIDELINES § 15300.2)
Categorical exemptions cannot apply where:
Unusual circumstances exist
Reasonable possibility of significant impacts exists
Cumulative impacts are present
Traffic diversion and emergency access changes may trigger exemption exceptions

-> VEHICLE MILES TRAVELED (VMT) ANALYSIS REQUIREMENT
CEQA Guidelines § 15064.3 requires VMT-based analysis
No VMT analysis provided
Network re-routing effects not evaluated

-> RELIANCE ON 2012 MASTER PLAN / OUTDATED BASELINE
- Environmental review must reflect current conditions, not 2012 assumptions
- No update analysis provided to account for:
Development changes
Traffic pattern shifts
Current roadway usage conditions

->GENERAL PLAN CONSISTENCY REQUIREMENT
- Government Code § 65402 requires explicit consistency findings
- Record does not clearly demonstrate:
Circulation element consistency
Mobility/access consistency
Safety policy alignment
PIECEMEALING PROHIBITION
CEQA prohibits segmentation of related actions
Project must be analyzed as a whole
Related Safe Routes / traffic calming actions may not be isolated

-> CUMULATIVE IMPACTS
- No cumulative analysis for: 
Nearby traffic calming measures
Network-wide circulation changes
Combined congestion/diversion impacts

-> ALTERNATIVES ANALYSIS (CEQA GUIDELINES § 15126.6)
No evaluation of feasible alternatives including:
Partial closure options
Signal timing modifications
Traffic calming without closure
Pilot or reversible design approaches

-> EQUITY AND ACCESS IMPACTS
No analysis of impacts on:
Seniors
Disabled residents
Caregivers
Service providers requiring direct access

-> EMERGENCY SERVICES OPERATIONAL STANDARD
- No documented review by fire, EMS, or police agencies
- No response time impact validation
- No operational routing assessment

-> RECORD ADEQUACY / SUBSTANTIAL EVIDENCE STANDARD
CEQA requires substantial evidence, not conclusions
Safety claims lack supporting:
Engineering studies
Traffic modeling
Environmental analysis

->> CONCLUSION
While safety and multimodal goals are legitimate, the current record is legally and technically insufficient
Approval should be deferred until completion of:
- CEQA determination, analysis
- VMT study
- Traffic and diversion analysis
- General Plan consistency findings
- Emergency services review
- Alternatives analysis
- Updated baseline environmental assessment

-> SUMMARY OF DEFICIENCIES 
The item raises multiple legal and procedural deficiencies: it may expose the City to CEQA liability and potential injunctive relief or invalidation if approval proceeds without proper compliance; it requires an explicit CEQA “project” determination under Pub. Resources Code § 21065; any categorical exemption would be improper if there are unusual circumstances or cumulative impacts under CEQA Guidelines § 15300.2; transportation analysis is deficient because it lacks required vehicle miles traveled (VMT) evaluation under CEQA Guidelines § 15064.3; reliance on a 2012 master plan is legally questionable without updated environmental baseline analysis; General Plan consistency findings are required under Gove
ADOPT a resolution to APPROVE a roadway modification associated with the McBryde Avenue Safe Routes to Park Project, including closure of the direct vehicular connection between Glenn Avenue and McBryde Avenue to improve traffic safety, reduce cut-through traffic, and support pedestrian and bicycle connectivity – Public Works Department (Hillal Hamdan 510-621-1612/Daniel Chavarria 510-620-5478).

agenda_briefs.title: Council to consider prioritizing 23rd Street Ciclovía Pilot Project
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond City Council will receive an update on the proposed 23rd Street Ciclovía Pilot Project and decide whether to prioritize it in the current Transportation and Public Works work plan. The pilot would close 23rd Street between Barrett Avenue and Rheem Avenue (0.8-mile segment) to vehicle traffic on Saturday, September 12, 2026, from 8:00 AM to 3:00 PM, integrated with the Fiestas Patrias Parade & Festival. Preliminary cost estimates range from $79,000 to $145,000. Council will consider staffing capacity, costs, trade-offs with other council priorities, and impacts on traffic safety, traffic calming, grant-funded projects, and resident traffic requests. This is a proposed project — no final decision has been made yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if prioritized, this could affect traffic, parking, and business loading/deliveries on 23rd Street on September 12, 2026.

key_dates.label: Proposed Ciclovía pilot event on 23rd Street

attachments.title: Implementing Wildfire Taskforce Recommendations Regarding Enforcing Weed Abatement Ordinance.pdf

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond Council to vote on wildfire weed abatement rules
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposal from the Wildfire Preparedness Ad Hoc Committee that would create new rules for clearing weeds and brush on properties in wildfire hazard zones. If approved, the city would: (1) shorten the appeal timeline for property owners who get a violation notice for not clearing defensible space (the buffer zone around a building that is kept free of flammable materials); (2) hire a contractor to do the work on properties where owners don't comply; and (3) create a one-year pilot program to help eligible homeowners pay for clearing weeds and brush within 0–30 feet of their structures, with priority given to hazards in Zone 0. The City Attorney's Office would have 90 days to write the actual bylaws (local laws) and bring them back for a final vote. Staff must also return within 60 days with a full implementation plan, including costs and funding sources.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision and the 60-day implementation plan — this could affect your property if you own land in a wildfire hazard zone.

key_dates.label: Staff implementation plan due to council
Proposed bylaws and program details due to council

agenda_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Agriculture, Construction & Development
agenda_briefs.source_body:
Q.7
Implementing Wildfire Taskforce Recommendation Regarding Enforcing Weed Abatement Ordinance
Attachments
|
Public Comments
1.
Implementing Wildfire Taskforce Recommendations Regarding Enforcing Weed Abatement Ordinance.pdf
ADOPT the recommendations of the Wildfire Preparedness Ad Hoc Committee and DIRECT the City Attorney's Office, in collaboration with the City Administration, to implement the following actions and return to the City Council within 90 days with any necessary ordinances, resolutions, contracts, budget appropriations, and additional implementation plans:
Defensible Space Appeal Process
Direct the City Attorney's Office to evaluate the current appeal process for enforcement of defensible space violations under the Richmond Municipal Code, including whether appeal timelines can be shortened for parcels located within the Local Responsibility Area (LRA) and the City's designated wildfire hazard zones, and return to the City Council with recommendations for any necessary amendments to the Richmond Municipal Code.
Vegetation Management Contract
Direct staff to establish an on-call vegetation management services contract for parcels within wildfire hazard zones where property owners fail to comply with defensible space requirements following all applicable enforcement procedures, and determine the appropriate department to administer and oversee the contract.
Resident Assistance Program
Direct staff to develop a one-year pilot Resident Assistance Program to assist eligible homeowners with weed abatement ordinance and defensible space improvements within 0–30 feet of structures, with priority given to hazards located within Zone 0, and return to the City Council with program guidelines, eligibility criteria, funding options, and implementation recommendations.
Implementation Report
Direct staff to return to the City Council within 60 days with an implementation plan, including proposed ordinances or policy amendments, estimated costs, potential funding sources, staffing requirements, and a proposed implementation schedule for each recommendation – Councilmember Soheila Bana (510-620-6743)/Councilmember Cesar Zepeda (510-620-6593).

Richmond GATE Act for Charter Amendment for November 2026 Ballot to establish Independent Ethics Commission.pdf

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond GATE Act ballot initiative to create an Independent Ethics Commission
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider placing the Richmond GATE Act on the November 2026 ballot. If approved, it would create a seven-member Independent Ethics Commission with subpoena power, a dedicated Investigator, and jurisdiction over elected officials, appointed officials, commissioners, lobbyists, contractors, and nonprofits doing business with the City. Year 1 funding is a mandatory $1,000,000 from the General Fund; Year 2 is the greater of $1,000,000 or 0.5% of General Fund revenues (~$1,620,000); Year 3+ is the greater of $1,000,000 or 0.5%–1.0% of revenues (~$1,620,000–$3,240,000). The Commission must be operational by June 1, 2027.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the July 21 council meeting — this is when the final vote to put the measure on the ballot is expected.

key_dates.label: Council vote to adopt ballot initiative resolution — Richmond GATE Act
Final deadline to adopt ballot resolution for November 2026 ballot
Independent Ethics Commission must be operational
